title: Christ as Prophet
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: Christ as Prophet - Psalm 23 - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Psalm 23 and the Prophetic Office of Christ
subpoints: The second in a three-part series: priest in Psalm 22, prophet in Psalm 23, king in Psalm 24
Christ as prophet primarily brings God's word to the people — this was the primary function, not future prediction
The Lord as shepherd rules, guides, feeds, and protects his sheep; sheep follow, obey, love, and trust
The litmus test of whether we are his sheep: do we hear his voice, obey it, follow him?

outline.title: The Lord is My Shepherd: Christ Provides All We Need (v. 1)
subpoints: Everything we need is given to us by Christ through his word as proclaimed faithfully in worship
We are not merely to attend worship — we are to be fully engaged as those actually feeding at the table
People who come without engagement are like dinner guests who sit at the table but refuse to eat
The promises of the world are empty because only the food given by the good shepherd satisfies the soul

outline.title: Green Pastures and Still Waters: Christ Leads Us Gently (vv. 2-3)
subpoints: Christ's yoke is easy and his burden is light; he leads us to places of rest, not arid desolate places
Sheep that stray from the shepherd are not safe — we have no natural defenses against the world, flesh, and devil
The command to flee sin and to gather together on the Lord's Day reflects the care of the shepherd
He restores the soul by preaching with authority, zeal, wisdom, and divine power to change hearts

outline.title: The Valley of the Shadow of Death: Christ Accompanies Us Through (v. 4)
subpoints: Every believer will walk through the valley of the shadow of death — there is no walking around it
It is the valley of the shadow of death, not death itself: for the Christian, death has no substantial evil
The shepherd walks through the middle of his flock speaking to calm them — Christ does the same for us
Nothing, not even death, will separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus (Romans 8)

outline.title: The Prepared Table and Overflowing Cup: Abundant Provision (v. 5)
subpoints: A Christian lacks nothing because Christ provides for his people — and abundantly
God cannot withhold any good thing from you that you need; if he gave you Christ, he will give you all things
The prosperity gospel is a lie from the pit of hell — God promises persecution, not ease
The testimony of Scottish Covenanters: when the storm blew hardest, the Lord's smiles were sweetest

outline.title: Dwelling in the House of the Lord Forever: Eternity as Our Hope (v. 6)
subpoints: Looking forward to eternity gives peace even in the most difficult circumstances
Christ endured the cross by looking to the joy set before him — martyrs died the same way
Setting affections on anything less than Christ and eternity with him is not worthy of a Christian
Five applications: hear Christ as prophet; consider how he shepherds you; obey his voice; ask him to teach you; wait on him

summary:
In the second installment of a three-part series on the Shepherd Psalms, Elder Nick Anderson preaches Psalm 23 through the lens of Christ's prophetic office. Just as Psalm 22 revealed Christ as our great high priest, Psalm 23 — the Lord is My Shepherd — reveals him as the prophet who brings God's word to his people, feeds them, leads them, and restores their souls. The elder notes that people commonly assume the prophet's primary role is predicting the future, but in Scripture it is to bring God's word to the people and call them to repentance — exactly what Christ continues to do through the faithful proclamation of the gospel every Lord's Day.

The sermon works through each verse of the Psalm. The great need expressed in verse 1 — 'I shall not want' — is met through the proclaimed word. The elder challenges the congregation not merely to attend worship but to be fully engaged in it, comparing passive attendance to sitting at a beautifully set dinner table and refusing to eat. Verses 2-3 show Christ leading his people to still waters and green pastures, restoring their souls — a picture of the gentle, patient work of the prophet-shepherd who knows his sheep well enough to know what they need and where to find it. The sermon draws a searching application: sheep without a shepherd have no natural defenses; Christians who neglect gathering together on the Lord's Day put their souls at risk.

Verse 4 — the valley of the shadow of death — receives particular attention. The elder notes it is a walk, not a run or a crawl, and that it is the shadow of death, not death itself, for the Christian has already passed from death to life. The shepherd's rod and staff comfort because his voice calms his sheep even in unfamiliar and frightening territory. The sermon concludes with the abundant provision of verse 5 (table prepared before enemies, cup running over), the testimony of Scottish Covenanters who rejoiced even in martyrdom, and verse 6's forward-looking hope of dwelling in the house of the Lord forever — the same hope that enabled Christ to endure the cross for the joy set before him.
transcript:
## Introduction

If you want to open your Bible to Psalm 23, that's where we will be tonight. Last week we covered Psalm 22 and so tonight I'm covering Psalm 23. And then next week we'll cover Psalm 24.

As I mentioned last week, these three Psalms have been historically grouped together. Some people have called them the Shepherd Psalms. One commentator said that Psalm 22 spoke of the past, Psalm 23 speaks of the present, and Psalm 24 speaks of the future. Another commentator said Psalm 22 is the servant shepherd, Psalm 23 is the good shepherd, and Psalm 24 is the ruling shepherd.

In this series I'm talking about Christ as our priest in Psalm 22, our prophet — which is what we're talking about tonight — in Psalm 23, and then next week we'll cover Christ as king in Psalm 24.

## Reading Psalm 23

*A Psalm of David. The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want. He makes me to lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside the still waters. He restores my soul. He leads me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake. Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil, for you are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me. You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. You anoint my head with oil; my cup runs over. Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life, and I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever.*

Certainly one of the most famous Psalms. For good reason. It's a great Psalm. I think it was Spurgeon who said: if Psalm 119 is like the giant of the Psalms, like the tallest tree, Psalm 23 is the flowers surrounding it.

## The Lord Is My Shepherd: Christ as Prophet

Verse 1 starts: the Lord is my shepherd. And Christ is the shepherd and we are his sheep. We could even talk about Christ as being priest in the way that he shepherds his people — he lays down his life for the sheep. The theme of shepherd, shepherding, and sheep is throughout Scripture. We see this in David himself, who was a shepherd; in the prophets who complained that the shepherds of Israel had led the sheep astray; and in Jesus who calls himself the good shepherd.

So if the Lord is our shepherd, what exactly does a shepherd do? He rules, he guides, he feeds, and he protects his sheep. And what do sheep do? They follow the shepherd. They obey him. They love him and they trust him.

So then we have to ask: how do we know that we are sheep? Do we hear his voice? Do we obey his voice? Do we follow him? If we have set our hearts on the Lord and following him, then we are his sheep. And remember what 1 John says: if we say we love him and we don't obey his commands, we are a liar. Obedience is the litmus test of our faith.

Now growing up I always thought the primary thing a prophet does is foretell future events. But when you actually read the prophecies, that's not really the case. Most of the time they're just telling people: this is what God's word says and you need to repent. This is what Christ does for us, even to this day. When we gather as his sheep, he protects us, he feeds us, he guides us.

### I Shall Not Want

The Psalm continues: I shall not want. So Christ as our shepherd and prophet knows exactly how to provide for us. David expresses this confidence: the Lord is my shepherd, therefore I shall not want. Everything that we need is given to us by Christ in his word. As the word is proclaimed to us every week, every Lord's day, Wednesdays, and on those special days we gather — what we need is given to us in the proclaimed word. We are in fact fed by Christ himself when his word is faithfully proclaimed.

What this means for us is that we should not merely attend worship services — we should be fully engaged in worship services. Imagine you are invited to someone's house for dinner. The table is set really nicely, you can smell the food, everything that you like. You sit down at the table and everyone starts eating and you just sit there doing nothing. You look at the food but don't taste it. You don't smell it. You don't drink anything. You just sit there wondering when this is going to be over so you can go home.

That's sometimes the way we treat our worship services. We come faithful to church but if we're not engaged in what we're doing, we're not actually feeding on the word. We're just watching other people eat and not benefiting from it. We're trying to feed you but you're refusing it.

Consider also that if Christ provides for you richly — which he does — everything that you need, then the promises of the world are completely empty. The temptation of money, prestige, worldly comfort — all these things are in vain. Nothing but the food given by the good shepherd can satisfy our souls.

## Green Pastures and Still Waters (vv. 2-3)

He makes me to lie down in green pastures. Christ's yoke is easy and his burden is light. This idea of lying down in green pastures carries the idea of relief from the heat of the sun. He doesn't make his flock lie down in arid, dry, hard places. He knows where the green pastures are and in his goodness he leads us to them.

Now, the places which seem to us to be the most difficult are frequently the places where we actually are fed the most, where we receive the most spiritual nourishment. So we ought to be thankful for those times. Even in the hardest of times we ought to be thankful, because the Lord is providing for us.

He leads me beside the still waters. He shows us the way and leads us. He leads us — and so we must follow him. A sheep that strays away from its shepherd is not safe. Sheep from what I've read don't have any natural defenses. There are actually only two things a sheep can do if endangered: run away, and gather with other sheep to try to protect themselves in the flock. This is you and me. We don't have any natural defenses against the attacks of the world, the flesh, or the devil. If we stray away from the shepherd, the only thing we can expect is difficulties.

And it's interesting that we're told two particular things: flee from sin, and do not forsake gathering together — especially on the Lord's Day. Apparently sheep are not keen on drinking from fast-flowing water. They prefer still water. So this picture of the shepherd guiding the sheep to still water shows his care — he knows his sheep, he knows they won't drink if they go to a fast-running stream. So he brings them to the still waters. And this is how Christ feeds us: he brings us to the still waters of the word so that we can feed and grow.

This is why we ought to never give up meeting together. I don't know anybody personally who ever stopped going to church and remained faithful in their walk. Not one. I read a book about World War II and a story stuck out to me — American GIs in a bombed-out city on a Sunday were stunned to see a family dressed in their Sunday clothes going to church. Those people understood how important it was to be in church.

He restores my soul. Christ preached in such a way that hearts were changed. He taught with authority — the people were astonished at his preaching because he taught with authority and not like the scribes. He preached with zeal. His preaching was accompanied by divine power to change hearts. He preached with wisdom so that nobody could contradict him. And today when the gospel is preached, Christ does exactly the same thing: he illuminates us, warms us, converts the unconverted, and sanctifies his people.

He leads me in paths of righteousness for his name's sake. The prophets were sent to guide the people back to the Lord — that was the greatest part of their ministry. Moses is called the greatest of all the prophets. What did he do? He led the people out of bondage and began to lead them to the promised land. And that's exactly what Christ does for us.

## The Valley of the Shadow of Death (v. 4)

Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death. Everyone will walk through the valley of the shadow of death. There is no walking around it. There is only walking through it. Notice too that it's a walk — not a run through, not a crawl. It's just a walk because the Lord is with us.

Notice it's not the valley of death. It's the valley of the shadow of death. And this is because death for the Christian can do no evil to us. There is no substantial evil in a Christian dying. That is our final victory over death when we die. And every true believer will walk through this valley and make it through to the other side.

In the Middle East, shepherds have to continually be moving the sheep from place to place. This makes sheep nervous — they get into unfamiliar places and become anxious. So what does the shepherd do? He walks through the middle of the flock, around the flock, and speaks to the sheep because the sheep know his voice and it calms them. That's a beautiful picture of what Christ does for his people when they're going through trials and suffering.

I will fear no evil for you are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me. There's two things in life that terrify people more than anything: death and public speaking. We don't have any promises about public speaking, but we do have promises about death — that the Lord is with us in that time. And like Paul we can say: O death, where is your sting? Where is your victory? And we can also say that nothing — not even death — will separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus.

## The Prepared Table and Overflowing Cup (v. 5)

You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. You anoint my head with oil; my cup runs over. A Christian lacks nothing because Christ provides for his people, and abundantly. There's no lack of good thing for God's people.

Romans 8:31-32: What then shall we say to these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son, but has delivered him up for us all — how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?

The Lord cannot withhold any good thing from you that you need. He can't — because he has already given you Christ and he's promised that every good thing you need he will give freely.

The prosperity gospel is a massive lie from the pit of hell. If you've bought into any part of it, reread Romans 8. He doesn't promise a life of ease. In fact, he promises we will be persecuted for our faith.

Let me share testimonies from two Scottish Covenanters. If you know anything about the Scottish Covenanters, they were fiercely persecuted for their faith. One was named John Cochran, a shoemaker, who was murdered, leaving a wife and six children. Before he died he left this testimony: 'There was no discouragement to me. For when the storm blew the hardest, the smiles of my Lord were the sweetest. It is a matter of rejoicing unto me to think how my Lord have passed by many a tall cedar and have laid his love upon a poor, grumble bush, the like of me.'

He didn't pity himself. He didn't complain that he was leaving his wife and kids behind. He actually rejoiced. And then another martyr who left his wife and children said: 'Now I commend my soul to God who has preserved me hitherto, and who unexpectedly singled me out to suffer for him, who am the unworthiest of all sinners. And I never thought he should have so highly privileged me as to account me worthy to give a testimony for him.'

If our brothers and sisters who went before us died rejoicing that they got to give a testimony for Christ, it's a rebuke to us when we're discouraged because our carburetor broke or because we have some disease or didn't get promoted.

## Dwelling in the House of the Lord Forever (v. 6)

And I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever. Looking forward to eternity gives us peace even in the most difficult circumstances. That is how Christ himself went through the cross — as he looked for the joy set before him. And that's how the martyrs endured their deaths — as they looked beyond the pain and suffering and saw that they would be with the Lord forever.

Setting our affections on anything less than Christ, anything less than being with Christ in eternity, is not worthy of a Christian. Colossians 3:1-4: If you then were raised with Christ, seek those things which are above where Christ is sitting at the right hand of God. Set your mind on things above, not on things on the earth, for you died and your life is hidden with Christ in God. When Christ who is our life appears, then you also will appear with him in glory.

## Five Applications

So what does this Psalm teach us beyond what I've already said?

First, we need to hear the prophet. We need to hear Christ. Matthew 17:5: 'This is my beloved Son in whom I am well pleased — hear him.'

Second, we need to consider how Christ shepherds us as a prophet. He shepherds us gently, kindly, wisely, and to suit us in whatever circumstances we find ourselves in.

Thirdly, if you're unsaved, listen to the voice of the shepherd and heed it.

Fourth, obey, Christian — obey the voice of the prophet, the good shepherd who laid down his own life for you.

Fifthly, ask him to teach you. Psalm 25:4-5: Show me your ways, O Lord; teach me your paths; lead me in your truth and teach me, for you are the God of my salvation; on you I wait all the day.

We need to wait on the Lord. We need to wait on him to teach us and then we need to make sure we're obeying the things he's teaching us.

*I am gracious Lord, we thank you for Christ, the good shepherd, our prophet. We thank you that you guide us, you lead us, you sanctify us, you protect us, you lead us to still waters where we can drink in safety, you lead us to green pastures where we find rest. And we ask you, Lord, to work in our hearts that we would love Christ more, that we would love your word more, that we would spend more time meditating on it, memorizing it, thinking about it through the day, feeding on it as we ought to. We pray that you would protect us, your sheep, from the attacks of Satan, protect us from our own sinful inclinations. We pray that you would sanctify us by your word tonight. And we ask these things in Christ's name. Amen.*
